proof_stages.label: Correlator integrands as symmetric conformal master objects
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets planar amplitude and correlator kinematics, defines the four-point half-BPS correlator integrand as a rational function of 4+L points, and emphasizes its full permutation symmetry and conformal weight as the master object whose limits contain amplitudes.

proof_stages.label: Planar f-graph encoding and older coefficient constraints
proof_stages.summary: The symmetric conformal integrands are encoded by planar f-graphs with denominator edges and possible numerator edges, while graphical constraints such as the square rule recursively determine many of their coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Cusp pinching rule for bootstrapping f-graphs
proof_stages.summary: A new double-triangle/cusp lightlike-limit rule gives linear relations between n-point and n-1-point graph coefficients, apparently fixing all planar f-graph coefficients through eleven loops and revealing striking zero and large-coefficient patterns.

proof_stages.label: Lightlike limits to amplitude squares
proof_stages.summary: Taking four, n, or all correlator points to form lightlike polygons extracts loop amplitude squares and tree-level helicity-summed amplitude squares, whose local x-space rational forms are simpler than direct BCFW squaring and motivate a direct squared-amplitude construction.

proof_stages.label: Positive-geometry and fibration picture
proof_stages.summary: The speaker discusses squared-amplituhedron and correlator positive geometries, then describes a practical fibration method that fixes four points, computes loop canonical forms over kinematic chambers, recovers correlators up to four loops, and raises symmetry and elliptic-cut issues.

proof_stages.label: Integration, identities, and periods
proof_stages.summary: After the integrands are obtained, integrating over loop or all points leads to polylogarithmic and elliptic functions, all-loop ladder and fishnet identities, and period sums constrained by localization and integrability, leaving higher-loop integration and zero-pattern questions open.
